creator: vsjcfm@gmail.com
text:
2011/2/24 Olli Hauer <ohauer@freebsd.org>:
> Hi Anton,
>
> It has nothing to do with the nmap version since this is how nmap is designed!
>
> If you are able to reconfigure MPD so it displays a default IP and not a netgraph
> route then I suspect you can use nmap with root privileges.
>
>> netstat -rn | grep default
>> default ng0 US 0 2809 ng0

Thank you for idea, I have changed default route from interface name
to my IP address.

root@jason:~# netstat -rn | grep default
default            193.xx.xx.74      US          0     3174    ng0

root@jason:~# nmap -F scanme.nmap.org
Starting Nmap 5.51 ( http://nmap.org ) at 2011-02-24 12:58 EET
Nmap scan report for scanme.nmap.org (64.13.134.52)
Host is up (0.21s latency).
Not shown: 95 filtered ports
PORT    STATE  SERVICE
22/tcp  open   ssh
25/tcp  closed smtp
53/tcp  open   domain
80/tcp  open   http
113/tcp closed auth

Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 7.82 seconds

All works normally. But I think that must be a little workaround for
getting IP of the interface-named routes.
In that case nmap will be able to determine correct route even the
interface's IP address will change without
editing MPD's configuration files. This will be useful for people with
dynamic adresses on VPN interfaces.
